title: How a Guitar Makes Sound
content: A plucked string vibrates at a frequency set by its length, tension, and mass, but a thin string alone barely moves any air. The string's energy travels through the saddle and bridge into the soundboard (acoustic) or is sensed by pickups (electric). The soundboard has far more surface area, so it pushes much more air and that moving air is what your ears hear. Heavier/longer strings vibrate slower (lower pitch); pressing a string to a fret shortens its vibrating length and raises the pitch.

title: Scale Length and Why It Matters
content: Scale length is the vibrating distance from the nut to the saddle (e.g., ~25.5" Fender, ~24.75" Gibson). At the same pitch, a longer scale needs higher string tension, which feels stiffer and sounds brighter and tighter; a shorter scale feels slinkier and sounds warmer and more compressed. Scale length also determines where the frets must be placed, so it directly affects intonation math. If a guitar feels harder or easier to bend than you expect, scale length and string gauge are usually why.

title: What Frets Do
content: Frets are metal strips placed at mathematically precise points that divide the string so each one raises the pitch by a half step. When you press behind a fret, the fret (not your finger) becomes the new endpoint of the vibrating string, fixing the exact pitch. Each fret up the neck moves ~5.95% closer to the bridge (the 12th fret sits at the exact midpoint and sounds one octave up). Worn, uneven, or lifting frets cause buzzing, dead notes, and intonation problems.

title: The Nut and Its Job
content: The nut is the slotted strip at the headstock end of the fretboard that sets string spacing and the height of the strings over the first few frets when played open. Each slot must be cut to the correct depth and width for its string and angled toward the tuners so the string breaks cleanly. If a slot is too low you get open-string buzz; too high makes the first few frets play sharp and feel hard to fret; too tight a slot causes tuning problems from binding.

title: Saddle and Bridge
content: The saddle and bridge anchor the strings at the body end and transfer their vibration into the soundboard (acoustic) or hold string height and intonation (electric). The saddle sets two things: action height (how high the string sits) and intonation (the exact front-to-back break point of each string). On electrics, individual saddle screws let you raise/lower height and move each saddle forward/back for intonation; on acoustics, action is changed by sanding the bottom of the one-piece saddle. A loose or cracked saddle can rattle and buzz.

title: Single Coil vs Humbucker Pickups
content: A pickup is a coil of wire around magnets that turns string vibration into an electrical signal. A single coil uses one coil and sounds bright, sharp, and twangy, but it picks up 60-cycle electrical hum from lights, transformers, and dimmers. A humbucker uses two coils wired in reverse with opposite magnetic polarity, which cancels that hum and gives a warmer, fuller, higher-output sound with a bit less treble sparkle. If your bright-sounding guitar hums and the noise changes when you rotate your body, you likely have single coils.

title: Tuning Machines (Tuners)
content: Tuning machines use a worm gear to wind the string around a post and hold tension precisely; higher gear ratios (e.g., 18:1) make fine tuning easier. Most tuning drift is NOT the tuners slipping but rather string binding at the nut, sloppy string winds at the post, or new strings still stretching. Make sure each string has two to three neat downward wraps on the post and that mounting screws are snug. Locking tuners clamp the string instead of wrapping it, which speeds string changes and improves stability, especially with a tremolo.

title: Acoustic Body Resonance
content: An acoustic guitar's top (soundboard) does most of the work, vibrating with the strings to move air; lighter bracing lets it vibrate more freely (more volume/bass) while heavier bracing tightens and focuses the tone. The hollow body plus soundhole acts as a Helmholtz resonator (like blowing across a bottle) that boosts low frequencies. Larger bodies (dreadnought, jumbo) enclose more air and produce deeper, louder bass; smaller bodies are more balanced and articulate. This is why body size and bracing change tone far more than the back/sides wood.

title: Fret Buzz: The Big Picture
content: Fret buzz happens when a vibrating string contacts a fret it shouldn't. The four main causes are: action too low, not enough neck relief (or a back-bowed neck), uneven/high frets, and nut slots cut too low. Technique (pressing too hard, slamming open chords, picking too hard for the setup) and using lighter strings than the guitar is set up for also cause it. Diagnose by WHERE it buzzes: open strings point to the nut, frets 1-5 to relief or low action, mid-neck (7-17) to relief, and a single fret to a high/uneven fret.

title: Localizing Where the Buzz Comes From
content: Pinpoint the source before adjusting anything. If a string buzzes ONLY when played open but is clean when fretted, the problem is the nut (slot too low). If it buzzes across the lower-to-mid frets when fretted, suspect neck relief (too little) or overall action being too low. If it buzzes on one specific fret and the notes below and above are clean, that next fret up is high or the buzzing fret is worn. Buzz everywhere usually means action is too low or the neck needs relief; buzz that only happens plugged in but not acoustically is electronics, not frets.

title: Fret Buzz from Action Too Low
content: If the strings sit too close to the frets, normal playing drives them into the frets and they buzz, often across many frets. Confirm by measuring action at the 12th fret (electric ~1.5-2mm; acoustic ~2mm treble to ~2.6mm bass). Fix by raising the saddles: on an electric turn each saddle height screw clockwise a little, on an acoustic you'd shim or replace the saddle. Remember that raising height at the saddle by a given amount only raises it about half that much at the 12th fret, so go in small steps and re-check.

title: Fret Buzz from Too Little Neck Relief
content: Strings vibrate in an arc, so the neck needs a tiny forward bow (relief) to give them room, mostly in the middle of the neck. If the neck is dead flat or back-bowed, you get buzz concentrated around frets 7-12. Check relief: capo the 1st fret, hold the low E down at the last fret, and look at the gap at the 7th-8th fret; it should be about the thickness of a thin business card (~0.008-0.012"/0.2-0.3mm). If there's no gap, add relief by loosening the truss rod (counterclockwise) a small amount.

title: Fret Buzz from High or Uneven Frets
content: If buzz is isolated to one or a few frets and relief/action/nut all check out, a fret is high, worn, or lifting. Test by fretting a note and checking the next fret up: with the string held down, a high fret will let the string touch the fret two positions ahead. A 'fret rocker' or short straightedge rocked across three frets will tee-totter on a high one. Small high spots can be leveled and re-crowned, but fret leveling/dressing or replacing a lifted fret is a job for a luthier to avoid ruining the fretboard.

title: Buzz at the Nut (Open Strings)
content: If a string buzzes only when played open and goes away the moment you fret any note, the nut slot for that string is cut too low and the string rattles on the first fret. Confirm: hold the string down at the 3rd fret and look at the gap over the 1st fret; you should see a sliver of daylight about the thickness of a sheet of paper. No gap means the slot is too low. Quick test/fix: slip a tiny shim of paper into the slot; if the buzz stops, the slot needs raising. Permanent fix is filling the slot (baking soda + thin superglue, or a new nut).

title: Truss Rod / Neck Relief Basics
content: The truss rod is a metal rod inside the neck that counteracts string tension to set neck relief. Turn it CLOCKWISE (righty-tighty) to straighten the neck and REDUCE relief; turn it COUNTERCLOCKWISE to add relief (more forward bow). Golden rules: move in tiny increments (1/8 turn or less), then re-tune and re-measure; if it won't budge or feels very stiff, STOP and see a tech. Most necks want a hair of relief, not a flat or back-bowed neck. Always check relief with the guitar tuned to pitch.

title: How to Measure Neck Relief
content: Tune to pitch. Put a capo at the 1st fret (or hold the low E there) and press the low E down at the fret where the neck meets the body (around the 14th-17th). The string now forms a straight line, so any gap in the middle is your relief. Look at the gap over the 7th-8th fret: aim for roughly 0.008-0.010" (about a thin business card). If the string sits flat on the frets with no gap, you have too little relief (add some). If the gap is large and the action feels high in the middle, you have too much (reduce it).

title: Adjusting Action Height
content: Action is string height measured at the 12th fret between the top of the fret and the bottom of the string, checked on both the high E and low E. Typical electric action is ~1.5-2mm; acoustics run a bit higher (~2.0-2.4mm treble, ~2.4-2.8mm bass). On an electric, raise/lower each saddle with its height screws (clockwise raises). Lower it until you hear buzz, then raise slightly until it's clean. Set neck relief FIRST, then action, then intonation, in that order, because each affects the next.

keywords: out of tune up the neck
title: Intonation: What's Wrong
content: Bad intonation means the guitar is in tune with open strings but plays progressively out of tune as you go up the neck, so open chords sound fine but barre chords or solos sound sour. Test it: tune the open string perfectly, then compare it to the note fretted at the 12th fret. If the 12th-fret note reads SHARP, the string's speaking length is too short; if it reads FLAT, it's too long. Fresh strings and correct relief/action should be set before intonating, since worn strings throw off the reading.

title: How to Set Intonation
content: Use a good tuner and play in your normal seated position. Tune the open string spot-on, then fret at the 12th fret and read the tuner. Rule: if the 12th-fret note is SHARP, move the saddle BACK (away from the neck, lengthening the string); if it's FLAT, move the saddle FORWARD (toward the neck). On most electrics you turn the saddle's intonation screw a small amount, then re-tune the open string and re-check the 12th fret. Repeat per string until the open note and 12th-fret note both read in tune. Acoustic intonation usually requires reshaping or replacing the saddle by a tech.

title: Why a Guitar Won't Stay in Tune
content: On a fixed-bridge guitar, tuning instability is almost always the nut or the string winds, not the tuners 'slipping.' The top culprits are: strings binding in the nut slots, too many sloppy wraps around the tuning post, brand-new strings still stretching, and old/dead strings. Diagnose by tuning up, doing a bend or two, and seeing if it drops flat (nut binding) or stays put. Fix the common cases: lubricate the nut slots, redo your string winds neatly (2-3 turns), and stretch new strings in. Temperature/humidity swings also detune wood necks.

title: Fixing Nut Binding
content: If a string makes a 'ping' or jumps in pitch when you tune or bend, it's catching in the nut slot instead of sliding. This is the single most common tuning-stability problem. Fix it with dry lubricant in the slot: graphite from a pencil tip, a dab of nut sauce/PTFE, or a touch of lip balm in a pinch. If it persists, the slot may be too narrow or rough and needs gentle widening, or you can switch to a self-lubricating nut (e.g., graphite/TUSQ XL). After lubricating, retune and bend a few times to seat the string.

title: New Strings Won't Stay in Tune
content: Brand-new strings keep going flat because they're physically stretching and settling under tension; this is normal, not a fault. Speed it up by gently pulling each string up away from the fretboard along its whole length a few times, then retuning, and repeating until the pitch holds. Neat winds (2-3 wraps spiraling downward on the post) help them seat. Give them a little playing time and they'll stabilize. If a string still won't hold after stretching, check the nut for binding and the post winds.

title: Do Locking Tuners Help?
content: Locking tuners clamp the string at the post instead of requiring multiple wraps, so there's almost no wrap to slip or stretch; this speeds string changes and improves stability, especially on guitars with a vibrato/tremolo. But they are NOT a cure-all: if your real problem is nut binding or a sloppy setup, locking tuners won't fix it. Address nut friction and proper winds first. They're a worthwhile upgrade for tremolo users and anyone who changes strings often.

title: Fixing a Guitar That's Hard to Play (High Action)
content: If the strings feel high and hard to press, especially up the neck, the action is too high. Work in order: first check neck relief (too much forward bow raises the middle of the neck, so reduce relief by tightening the truss rod a touch); then lower the saddle height at the bridge; finally check the nut height if the first few frets feel stiff. Lower in small steps and play-test after each change, stopping just before buzz appears. If you hit buzz before it feels comfortable, the neck or frets may need work.

title: Changing Strings: The Basics
content: Loosen and remove strings one at a time (or in pairs) to keep tension on the neck. Thread the new string through the bridge/tailpiece, pull it up to the correct tuner, and leave 2-3 inches of slack past the post. Wind so the wraps stack DOWNWARD (toward the headstock face) in 2-3 neat turns, keeping tension on the string; this break angle improves tuning stability. Snip the excess, tune up, then stretch the strings in. Wipe strings down after playing to make them last longer.

title: Buzzes and Rattles That Aren't Fret Buzz
content: Not every buzz comes from the frets. Loose tuner nuts/screws, a loose truss-rod nut, a string end vibrating against the headstock, a loose saddle or bridge pin, a loose strap button, or (on acoustics) a loose brace or pickguard can all rattle. To find it, play the note that triggers the buzz and press on suspects one at a time (touch the headstock, hold the truss-rod cover, mute string ends) and listen for it to stop. Tighten hardware finger-snug; an internal acoustic brace buzz needs a luthier.

title: Amp Input/Gain Basics
content: The gain (or 'pre') knob sets how hard you drive the amp's preamp stage: low gain stays clean, high gain overdrives the preamp tubes into distortion. The master volume (or 'post') controls overall loudness from the power section. This split lets you get a distorted preamp tone at low volume, or a cleaner preamp tone loud. If your tone is muddy and fizzy, you may have too much gain; if it's thin and you want grit, raise gain (or add an overdrive pedal) rather than just turning up volume.

title: Troubleshooting Amp Hum and Buzz
content: Isolate the cause step by step. First turn up the amp with NOTHING plugged in: if it hums, the issue is the amp or the outlet/grounding. Plug in just a cable (no guitar): a buzz now points to a bad/unshielded cable, so swap it. Plug in the guitar and stop touching the strings: if hum gets louder, that's single-coil/electronics noise or poor shielding. Plug all gear into one power strip to avoid ground loops, keep away from lights/dimmers and computer monitors, and try rotating your body, single coils pick up interference directionally.

title: Single-Coil Hum (and How to Tame It)
content: Single-coil pickups naturally pick up 60-cycle electromagnetic hum, and it gets worse near fluorescent/LED lights, dimmers, computer monitors, and with high-gain or distortion settings. Confirm it's the pickups by touching the strings or metal hardware: if the hum drops, your guitar is acting as an antenna and grounding through you. Reduce it by moving away from interference sources, rotating to find a quieter angle, shielding the control cavity with copper foil, or switching to noiseless single-coils or humbuckers. Some hum is inherent to the single-coil design.

title: Diagnosing a Bad Cable or Jack
content: Crackling, signal cutting out, or loud buzz when you wiggle the cable usually means a failing cable or a loose output jack, not the amp. Test by swapping in a known-good cable; if the noise goes away, the cable was bad. If wiggling the plug AT THE GUITAR causes it, the output jack is likely loose, tighten the jack nut (don't let the internal wires twist). Use well-shielded cables with solid metal plugs, and keep cables away from power cables to reduce induced hum.

title: How Delay Pedals Affect Tone
content: A delay pedal records your signal and plays it back as one or more repeats (echoes), set by delay time, feedback (number of repeats), and mix level. Short delays thicken/double your sound, long delays create rhythmic echoes, and high feedback builds washy ambience. Place delay near the END of your chain, after overdrive/distortion and modulation, so it echoes your finished tone instead of distorting the echoes. If your amp has an effects loop, putting delay there keeps it clean and clear.

title: How Reverb Pedals Affect Tone
content: Reverb simulates the sound of a physical space, the dense reflections you'd hear in a room, hall, plate, or spring, adding depth and a sense of dimension. It should go LAST in the signal chain (or in the amp's effects loop) so all your other effects sit 'inside' that space; putting reverb before distortion makes the wet reverb tails get distorted and sound harsh and muddy. Use a little for natural ambience or a lot for big atmospheric washes. Many amps have built-in reverb (often spring-style).

title: How an Acoustic Piano Works
content: Pressing a key swings a felt-covered hammer that strikes the tuned steel strings for that note, then the hammer immediately bounces away so the strings can ring. The strings' vibration passes through a bridge into the large wooden soundboard, which moves much more air and is what you actually hear. A piano has more strings than keys, around 230, because higher notes use 2-3 strings each for fuller tone while the lowest notes use one thick wound string. Damper felts rest on the strings and lift only while a key is held, stopping the note when you release.

title: The Piano Action and Touch
content: The 'action' is the lever mechanism that translates a key press into a hammer strike. Its key trick is escapement (let-off): the hammer is thrown at the string and released just before contact, so even if you hold the key down the string can vibrate freely. A good action also resets quickly for fast repeated notes. Because the hammer is physically thrown, how fast you press the key controls how hard the hammer hits, which is why an acoustic piano responds so sensitively to your touch from a whisper to a bang.

title: Dampers and Piano Pedals
content: Each note has a damper, a felt pad that sits on the strings to silence them; pressing a key lifts that note's damper, and releasing drops it to stop the sound. The right pedal (sustain/damper) lifts ALL dampers at once so notes ring after you release the keys, the most-used pedal. The left pedal (una corda/soft) shifts the action so the hammer hits fewer strings for a softer, mellower tone. The middle pedal (sostenuto, when present) sustains only the notes held at the moment you press it.

title: Digital vs Acoustic Piano
content: An acoustic piano makes sound mechanically (hammers, strings, soundboard) and needs tuning; a digital piano plays high-quality recorded/modeled samples through speakers and never needs tuning. The most important spec on a digital piano is the keyboard action: look for fully weighted, hammer-action keys so the feel and finger technique transfer to a real piano. Digitals add volume control, headphones, multiple voices, and recording, but even good ones don't fully match an acoustic's dynamic subtlety. For serious classical study, choose weighted hammer action at minimum.

title: Weighted vs Synth-Action Keys
content: Fully weighted (hammer-action) keys use real moving weights/hammers to mimic an acoustic piano's resistance, best for piano technique. Synth-action keys are light and spring-loaded, snapping back fast, which suits organ, synth leads, and fast electronic playing but feels 'lifeless' to a trained pianist. Semi-weighted sits in between, using springs plus a little added weight. Choose based on what you play: pianists want weighted; synth/organ players and producers often prefer synth-action for speed. Many people find the 'wrong' action surprisingly awkward.

title: MIDI Controllers and Velocity Sensitivity
content: A MIDI controller has keys but usually NO internal sounds, it sends note/velocity data over USB/MIDI to a computer or sound module that produces the actual audio, which is why a controller alone is silent until connected to software. Velocity sensitivity means the keyboard measures how fast/hard you strike a key (MIDI velocity 0-127) and sends that, so soft presses are quiet and hard presses are loud/brighter. If your controller plays at one flat volume, check that velocity sensitivity is on and the velocity curve fits your touch. Controllers often add pitch/mod wheels, pads, and knobs that digital pianos lack.

title: Correct Order for a Full Setup
content: A guitar setup must be done in order because each step affects the next. The sequence is: 1) fresh strings tuned to pitch, 2) set neck relief with the truss rod, 3) set action height at the saddles, 4) set/adjust nut slot height if needed, then 5) set intonation last. Doing intonation before relief and action, for example, wastes the work because changing relief or height shifts the intonation again. Make one small change at a time, retune, and re-check before moving on.

title: String Gauge and Buzz/Setup
content: Changing string gauge changes tension, which changes how much the neck bows and how the guitar is set up. Going to LIGHTER strings lowers tension, lets the neck straighten (less relief), and the floppier strings vibrate wider, so you often get new buzz and may need to add relief and/or raise action. Going HEAVIER adds tension and forward bow and usually requires adjusting the truss rod and re-checking intonation. Whenever you switch gauge significantly, expect to redo relief, action, and intonation.

title: When the Buzz Is Your Technique
content: Some buzz is player technique, not hardware. Press firmly and place your fingertip just BEHIND the fret (not on top of it or far back in the middle of the space) so the string seats cleanly. Buzzing only on certain notes you fret, or only when you dig in hard with the pick while the guitar plays clean otherwise, points to technique or a pick attack too heavy for a low action. Before adjusting the truss rod or saddles, have someone else play it or check that fretting closer to the fret and pressing a bit harder clears the buzz.

title: Humidity and Seasonal Neck Changes
content: Wooden necks absorb and release moisture, so humidity swings change neck relief and action over the seasons, this is a leading cause of buzz or high action that 'appears' with no other change. In dry winter air the wood can shrink and the neck back-bow (causing buzz); in humid summer it can swell and raise the action. The fix is often a small seasonal truss-rod tweak and keeping acoustics in 40-50% relative humidity with a case humidifier. If your guitar suddenly buzzes or stiffens with the weather, check relief before assuming something broke.
